title: How Shall an Abolitionist Vote?
author: Sam Jones
orientation: Sam Jones argues that an abolitionist's voting decision must be governed by God's law rather than situation ethics or pragmatic incrementalism, and concludes that supporting candidates like Donald Trump who openly oppose abolishing abortion is not a faithful option.
audiences: Christian voter weighing the lesser-of-two-evils argument
Pro-life incrementalist who treats political pragmatism as compassion
Abolitionist looking for a biblical framework for moral dilemmas
Pastor or ethics teacher engaging Joseph Fletcher's situation ethics

questions.question: What two convictions does Jones say must define how an abolitionist approaches the ballot box?
questions.audience: Abolitionist looking for a biblical framework for moral dilemmas
questions.answer: Jones grounds the entire essay on the claim that an abolitionist is first a Christian and second an immediatist, and that both identities must dominate every decision—including voting—rather than being set aside under political pressure.
questions.quote: The first is that an abolitionist is a Christian, and the second is that an abolitionist is an immediatist. Both of these should weigh heavily in the hearts and minds of abolitionists as they eat or drink and certainly as they go to the voting booth.

questions.question: Why does Jones identify Joseph Fletcher's situation ethics as a humanistic framework rather than a Christian one?
questions.audience: Pastor or ethics teacher engaging Joseph Fletcher's situation ethics
questions.answer: He points to Fletcher's own credentialing by humanist organizations to argue that situation ethics—though taught from divinity school chairs—was recognized by humanists as their own project, which is why it cannot serve as a Christian moral framework.
questions.quote: In 1974, Fletcher was named Humanist of the Year by the American Humanist Association, and in 1983, Humanist Laureate by the Academy of Humanism.

questions.question: How does Jones connect situation ethics to the political logic of incrementalism?
questions.audience: Pro-life incrementalist who treats political pragmatism as compassion
questions.answer: He argues both share the pragmatist creed that the ends justify the means, which is why every gestational-age ban or heartbeat bill can be defended with the same 'one life saved' rhetoric situationists use to defend voting compromises.
questions.quote: The ends justifying the means is, of course, the heartbeat of incrementalism, “If it just saves one life, it is worth it.” The plea of pragmatism is what is behind every 20-week ban, 12-week ban, and fetal heartbeat bill that has ever been presented.

questions.question: Why does Jones reject 'What is most loving?' as the operative question in moral decision-making?
questions.audience: Christian voter weighing the lesser-of-two-evils argument
questions.answer: Because the very same question, in Fletcher's hands, was used to justify infidelity, lying, abortion, and infanticide—proving that 'most loving' becomes whatever a compromised conscience prefers in the moment, with deadly consequences for the pre-born.
questions.quote: You see, Fletcher used love to justify infidelity, lying, abortion, and infanticide. To him, it all depended on the situation; then you would weigh out the consequences, and finally, you would compromise to a place where it would seem to be the “most loving.”

questions.question: What single question does Jones say a Christian must ask in every moral dilemma?
questions.audience: Abolitionist looking for a biblical framework for moral dilemmas
questions.answer: Rather than searching for middle ground, weighing outcomes, or calculating love, the Christian must fix on the question of what God has commanded and then demand obedience first of himself and then of others.
questions.quote: A Christian must approach every moral dilemma with a laser-like focus on the question, “What is right according to God?” And then the Abolitionist must demand obedience to that standard.

questions.question: How does Daniel's refusal of the king's meat function as a model for voting decisions?
questions.audience: Abolitionist looking for a biblical framework for moral dilemmas
questions.answer: Jones points to Daniel as the template for uncompromised conviction: he didn't barter, didn't negotiate consequences, and didn't soften the standard even when others would face danger because of it.
questions.quote: Daniel didn’t approach the matter asking if the ends would justify the means, he didn’t try to make some kind of compromise only eating the king’s food on Tuesdays and Thursdays, and most assuredly didn’t ask what would be the most loving thing to do for himself or his boss (the chief eunuch). Daniel clearly asked the question, “What has God declared right?”

questions.question: What four biblical criteria does Jones say every vote must measure a candidate against?
questions.audience: Christian voter weighing the lesser-of-two-evils argument
questions.answer: Drawing from Exodus 18:21, Jones insists every vote be cast for someone who is able, who fears God, who speaks truth, and who hates covetousness—criteria that disqualify candidates the political class treats as electable.
questions.quote: Every vote you cast ought to be in submission to this command, and every candidate you vote for ought to be 1. Able, 2. Fear God, 3. Speakers of truth, and 4. Hate covetousness (not given to bribes).

questions.question: Why does opposing the abolition of abortion automatically disqualify a candidate in Jones's framework?
questions.answer: Because the fear of God is inseparable from protecting the innocent and defending the fatherless, and the pre-born sit at the top of both lists—so any candidate openly against abolition fails the most basic test, regardless of electoral consequences.
questions.quote: To fear God, one must protect the innocent and defend the fatherless. Our pre-born neighbors not only qualify but are first on both of those lists. Any candidate that openly opposes abolishing abortion is not worthy of your vote.

questions.question: What is Jones's final verdict on voting for Donald Trump?
questions.answer: He closes by stating plainly that the abolitionist framework leaves no room to support Trump unless he repents, and calls readers instead to pray for that repentance while staying focused on rescuing the pre-born.
questions.quote: There is no room within the Abolitionist framework to vote for men like Donald Trump, unless, of course, they repent. So let us remain faithful to God, pray for President Trump’s repentance, and burn with a holy fire to rescue our pre-born neighbors.

related.title: Why I Believe Voting for a Pro-Abortion Candidate is a Sin, No Matter the Context
related.reason: Direct continuation of the same thesis—voting for a pro-abortion candidate is sin regardless of the lesser-of-two-evils calculus.

related.title: Christians could force Trump to be an abolitionist of abortion with merely a lift of the finger. #YouDontGetMyVoteYET
related.reason: Companion piece published two days earlier responding to the same Trump 'states' rights' statement that triggered Jones's article.

related.title: A Mother Is a Magistrate: Why Duress Is No Defense for Abortion
related.reason: Same author (Sam Jones) applying the same uncompromising biblical-law framework to another moral dilemma in the abolition movement.

related.title: Immediate, Not Gradual
related.reason: Foundational page on immediatism—the second of the two convictions Jones names as defining for abolitionist voting.

related.title: Incrementalism Versus Immediatism in a Nutshell
related.reason: Concise framing of the incrementalism-vs-immediatism divide that sits underneath Jones's voting argument.

related.title: Five Reasons Abolitionists Oppose Incremental Pro-Life Bills
related.reason: Extends Jones's pragmatism critique from the ballot box to the legislative compromises he names (heartbeat bills, 12- and 20-week bans).

related.title: Four Problems With David Barton's Incrementalism Apologia
related.reason: Rebuts the Christian intellectual case for incrementalist political compromise that Jones is implicitly attacking.
